speaker: interviewer
text: This Is the beginning of tape number six in an interview with Emanuel Mittelman. OK. When we left off, you were in the chicken coop, having just survived an air raid by the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm in a chicken coop, and everything quiets down. I'd like to mention that this Nazi commandant that we had on this train who was constantly warning us that every Jew will be killed. If something should happen to this train, no Jew will come alive. And we used to wear on our uniforms a triangle that identified us as Jew. As a matter of fact, I have it with my number two on my chest. There's a triangle. It's a clear triangle showing that I'm Jewish. A red one was political. Green was different-- was [NON-ENGLISH], whatever. And that plain triangle was that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I began thinking if this guy is going to kill all the Jews, how do I-- how do I get out of this dilemma? Maybe I have a certain way of protecting myself. So I started mingling with the non-Jewish guys, and I tore off my triangle from my uniform, pretending I am maybe Polish. I spoke Polish fluently at that time. And I mingled with that crowd, just in case of something happened at the end of it and this guy is going to kill all the Jews, maybe I have a way how to escape. So here I'm in the chicken coop. And I'm beginning to think, here I am in my own country, in a country where I was born. I speak the language. I have civilian clothes on me. Do I go back, or do I stay here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's a Nazi commandant waiting to kill all the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know what to decide. Meanwhile, I'm full with these-- with these-- with these warm eggs, nauseating from it, and I don't know what to do. And I hear-- I hear commotion because this farm wasn't too much away from the train. And I hear commotion. And I decide in my mind that it would be better if I go back for the simple reason that if I don't go back, and the first guy who meets me and he gives me out to the German, I'm for sure dead. I mean, there's no doubt about that. So if I go back, maybe I have a chance that I stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not wanting to do it, but I did it anyway, I started walking back. And the first thing what I did is I went to the place to see how my friend is doing, the one I left before, [SOBBING] I find him dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really couldn't take it because it hurt me so much, because I tried to persuade him to all my might that he should come away, that this is an unsafe place. And he wouldn't listen. So [SIGH] I had to-- I had to take this very hard because he was one of my friends that I knew all the time, [SOBBING] and he was one of the few who still had a number on the chest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I come out, I see that the Czech population is coming in from the city and bringing food to all the people. So I went out there too. And we began mingling, and the SS by now permitted them to hand us the food. But they wanted to give it to one gathering place, and then they'll distribute it. So we had a little chat with them. We talked to them too. It was possible just to throw a few words in. And meanwhile, we get the good news that the-- our Nazi commandant who was there with the two horses-- he got shot in both feet, in both legs. And they had to take him away with an ambulance to the hospital. So we're, like, there was a-- a-- a sigh of relief because we thought, oh, death penalty, it was just removed from all the Jews. And a couple of his horses also got shot. They were not dead, but they got shot. So in all the mish-mash that was going around there-- you can imagine, after a shooting and things, and the damages, and the trains were shot, and the-- with the civilian people bringing food-- someone of the SS men didn't want the horses to suffer. He shot them dead. And they gave us permission that everybody who wants to can take a piece of meat from the-- from the horses. So it took them I don't know how many hours, you know, to distribute the food. And we at least ate for a while well, which wasn't so healthy either because once you stuff your stomach after you didn't eat for so long, you usually become sick. And they told us that within a certain time the locomotive will be repaired. We'll get a second locomotive, and we'll be moving on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so after they gave us permission that everybody can take along some-- from the horses the meat, so we all ran there. We didn't have any knives. I don't remember how we cut a piece of meat, but somehow we got a hold of a piece of horse meat. And a friend of mine found at the station a big sack of probably 50 or 100 kilo dried onions. I don't know what you know what dried onions is. They used to sell them at that time. So we took a lot of this meat from the horse on the train, and we took the whole sack of the dried onions in our wagon. And I came back to the-- my old Jewish friends there because by now, I wasn't afraid anymore, you know, for my life as I was before, because I thought, well, if the guy's removed, maybe the cloud of-- of-- of the threat of the Jewish people is now diminished. And we boarded the plane-- the train, rather-- and we took off eventually from this place. And in the train we had a little more space, for some reason. I remember we could sit down. But we had no wood or no fire to heat up the meat. So we-- everybody was satisfied, satiated at that time from the food that the people, the civilians, brought, but thought for later on we have now an iron ration, so to call, save for the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the next days, what we were working-- everybody was peeling with their nails chips of wood from the side of the train. They were all from wood. Everybody had a job to peel. I don't know if you ever watched a movie that-- that-- they-- they-- they-- where the guy was chipping with a little nail file every day a little bit piece of cement there, until he made a hole. So eventually, we accumulated enough wood. Somebody had some matches. We lit some matches, and we took some of the meat. We used to heat it up, you know, with a match for two seconds. I mean, it wasn't cooked, but with some dried onions and some meat, we had a few days-- we had food as we were going on this plane-- this train, rather. I don't know why I'm always mixing up 'plane' with 'train.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And we kept on being on this train day in, day out again, moving in, until we wandered into-- now I'm talking about we are already in the end of, the midst of April 1945. And they-- they brought us into Austria. And one day, probably they had no more way-- places where to go-- they caught up because we were not too far away where the American lines were fighting already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put-- took the whole train one day, and they put us with the SS that were guarding us on a [SPEAKING GERMAN], which is a German word for a dead-end track. It's a track where it's-- it does not interfere with the traffic of-- of-- all the other trains. And if you want to put away for repairs a few of this details there from the train, you put them in a place like this, and it's not being bothered-- it doesn't bother anybody. And that's the way we were sitting day in and day out. The only food that we had is, like I said, the leftovers from this. No water, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day, of course, we hear-- we hear the-- the-- the shooting, right and left. We were--we knew we are in the-- in the front lines. We didn't know exactly where we were. And the SS men come out from the trains every day to taunt us a little bit, to heckle us, you know, to heckle us and to make some remarks and something like this. And I remember like today that on the 30th of April, one of them came and says through the window that Hitler was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought the guy's trying to get us to be happy and to show some joy, and then he'll take out his rifle and he'll do away with us. So we were very, very reserved. Nobody reacted to that. And then he started to, you know, it's true. It's true. Because all of us spoke German, of course, at that time. He says, it's true, you know, he's dead. He's dead. And pretty soon the war will be over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we didn't trust him. But what they did do is they did used to go there someplace and bring us some water. They were probably lacked of food of themselves by that time. They didn't have too much either. Like I said, there wasn't a place where they could get-- draw some-- some supplies. And it was April the second or April the third that was like we were hanging in limbo. We were sitting there. Didn't know should we rejoice, should we be happy. But all of a sudden, it began inside to work. [? Hey, ?] there is ray of hope. I mean, we-- we-- from all the shootings that we heard, we hoped that maybe, maybe we can come out from this and-- and see again the daylight. And the next day, the-- the guards start going around each of the-- of the wagons there and telling us that-- who would want to come out. They're opening the door. They want somebody should be an escort to them, to come along to the American lines and tell the Americans that they were very nice to us, that they protected us, that they were good to us. Of course, nobody wanted to do it because first of all, we're afraid the first one jumps out of the train, he'll take the rifle and bingo. And that's it, trying to escape. So we were very reluctant to give in or-- or show any kind of emotions to them, and we were just playing the waiting game. You know, the shooting was going on. We were here starving to death, hungry, thirsty, and-- and we didn't know what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the fifth of May-- on the fifth of May, we get up in the morning. There's no SS guard. We look out. Nobody sees an SS guard. We used to yell, we'll yell over to the other-- hey, fellows, does anybody see a guard? Nobody sees a guard. So little by little, one or two guys started jumping out of the train, one by one, one by one, one--. No guard. Nobody's being shot. Nobody's--. So by the time we saw that it's really the-- you know, everything is clear, so somebody started yelling on top of his-- of his lungs, hey, fellow, we're free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't have to explain you what happened at that minute. You know, we broke up in a jovial dance and crying. It's a funny thing that then, more than ever, at that time we were crying for happiness than smiling and laughing for happiness. That every second that we felt [SOBBING] that this is it. This is freedom. The-- the tears were pouring from the eyes like crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got together, and we said, well, let's-- let's-- let's go find some Americans. Let's do something because we haven't reached, you know, the complete freedom. We weren't sure. We were out. We have no guards. Nobody's shooting, which is fine. So now we started walking towards the sound of the shooting. So we approach, and-- and within an hour or so, we approached the front line of an American brigade, you know, the panzer division. The guys who are really, you know, with their helmets, with bullets, billion-- billions of bullets around them with-- you know, with the-- on the bands. Machine guns and-- and-- and full of dust. They're the first lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we come running. These guys looking bewilderment, and we fall on these guys and start kissing them and kissing the tanks and licking the dust from their shoes for happiness. And, you know, nobody speaks English. That's a problem. And we talk to these guys, and we talk to the guys, and we-- no communication. But they-- they see what's going on. So they take out their iron rations, and they feed us some food. The problem was that they gave us these cans with-- with ham and some stuff which was awful for a person whose stomach hasn't eaten for-- things. And we were eating like the pigs at the time, really. But the emotional thing, when we saw these guys, that the guys didn't know what's going on with us. They thought that we were crazy. We hugged them. We kissed them. We just couldn't show enough appreciation because we couldn't express it. So we expressed it with our arms and with our-- with our yelling to them, and-- whatever. I mean, they were going nuts in the-- here in the middle there in the front lines. And their commandant is yelling, and they're getting radio messages that they-- we cannot stay there. You know, we're being shot at. The-- the war wasn't the-- the end till-- until, I think, the ninth of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, they took us, and they detailed us to some guys in the field who had little tents there. They let us sleep there, and they gave us some food. And-- we're breathing-- we're free, we're really free. I mean, you know, you try to touch with your hand to feel the air. All of a sudden that-- that-- that-- that a dream like this came true. You really-- there's no SS to shoot at you. So I was sitting with a-- with a couple of guys, with Americans. And a friend of mine who I befriended on the train-- he was in the French Foreign Legion. He was much older than I am. And this guy spoke Spanish. No, he spoke French.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he found one of the soldiers also spoke French. So somehow they took us in in the tent there, and we stayed there about 24 hours with them. You know, and they were playing host to us. They was giving us whatever you want, cigarette, chocolate. You know, they had-- this army in their-- in their front lines, they have anything. And then they got a phone call one day on they-- on their field telephones that we cannot stay with them. They have to get rid of us because we can get shot still by the oncoming fire from the enemy. There's no armistice yet, so--.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us, I'll tell you what. We can't just send you away like this. So they looked at the street, and they saw three Germans riding on horses without saddles, just like this. So they say, you know how to ride a horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, 'of course' was a big lie, but I said 'of course.' Didn't have anything else to move with. So he and I and one more guy, they took away the horses from the Germans. And we sat down on the horses, and we started riding on the horse. There's no saddle. And he says, keep on going. And it was-- you know, we kept on riding until we came into the next village, whatever. Now we already eaten, and we feel good. So now we have to find a place where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we come into a little village, and-- and the shooting was still going on. You hear-- constantly you heard the flak. And we come in in a place where there's a beer house, you know, like a place where they go to drink beer, a whole bunch of American soldiers drinking beer. And one of them drunk like a pig pulls out his .38, comes over to me as I sit here. He puts it to my temple, and he starts yelling, Nazi! Nazi! I said to him, what are you talking about? I have a problem speaking English. I couldn't-- I-- I-- I-- I-- the only new word I knew is to yell to him, Jew, me Jew, me Jew, me Jew. No, you're Nazi. I'm going to shoot you. You Nazi. Here I'm standing, and I'm kneeling down to him, and I'm praying. I say, I went through all these three years through camp, and I'm going to find my death in a bullet to my head through an American soldier? To American soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pouring my heart out to the guy in German as much as I could. He finally let me go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was like a miracle. I slept over there in-- I don't remember where-- in some-- in some-- in some place there with the other two guys too. Eventually we ended up in-- in-- in a-- in a DP camp in Linz. Linz. It's a city in Germany, and the Americans had their own gathering DP camp. They came in there, and they bathed us and gave us supplies, whatever. I mean, I mean, nothing to-- to wear, but as far as eating, and I don't know, they checked us over. They had a doctor that-- to look us over to see how good we feel. And they told us we have to wait for a period of time until they'll be able to repatriate us, send us to each to the country where you come from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, we were young, and now the whole world standing in front of you. And you think, I have time to sit here to wait till they're going to send me home? I'm not going to sit here. There's no such a thing. So I tell my friend who was in the Foreign Legion. I picked up some French because I was with a lot of French guys in concentration camp. And by that time, my accent was terrific, and-- and-- and-- and I spoke maybe a couple hundred words, which is enough on the street to get along. So he said to me, I'll tell you what. They're going to repatriate me to Paris. He says, I'm going to teach you a certain number what you can tell them and the name and so forth, and give a little French accent, he says. And you will tell them you were also in the French Legion, and you'll come with me to Paris. So he gave me an address and, you know, from French, from Paris and so forth. I still remember it to today. And-- and-- and-- and I passed the whole business. And here I'm sitting under a bomber in-- in-- in Linz on the air strip by the American army, and they're supposed to fly us to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine. I have a pajama shirt on and short pants. Not pants, even, like-- like-- like-- like, I would say, like-- like shorts. You know, like-- like briefs. That's-- that's what I had on. Nothing else. No coat, nothing. Nothing in my hand, just a piece of paper I did have-- that I-- that they-- that I come from this DP camp. And I'm sitting with this friend there waiting, and one plane after another is taking off, taking guys to-- to-- to different places. The guy, they have priorities. Anybody who was in the Foreign Legion is like a soldier. So they were-- they were repatriate them much faster than the rest of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I'm sitting with this guy and talking. And I'm happy. I'm going to-- to France. I'm going to be-- in couple hours, I'm going to be in Paris. So a Jeep comes by again, calls my name out, and takes me away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, he said something there in English, but I didn't understand a word he was saying, why he is taking me away. Maybe he found out that my number wasn't right or whatever it was. And bingo, he takes me back to that place. He releases me there, and I'm back in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm not going to sit and wait there. And I pick myself up, and the world there really was in topsy-turvy. You know, everybody was moving about the best way you could.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the law was passed right away there in Austria and in Germany and in Czechoslovakia that anybody who was in a camp and he presents a number, and I had identification card from this place there-- has to be helped with any kind of-- of vehicle, train, free of charge. In-- they had to pick up-- put up big stations from Red Cross to feed us and so forth. So I kept on going on foot and on-- and on hitching rides in, you know, with-- with-- with army vehicles, because very, very little private stuff was going on. And then till I reached some train. And then the train-- it was just a question of going up and showing my identification DP number. Bingo, I was-- you know, I could travel. And little by little, I make my way till Bratislava. It took a while, I mean, a few days, nights. And on the road we found people that we met from before, that you knew from before the war. And needless to say that every time you found somebody you haven't seen them for years and that he is still alive, you know, you were hugging, you were kissing. The-- the-- the welcome was unlike it is nowadays today when you meet a friend. And everything was, like I mentioned, mixed with tears because our joy was-- was-- was to such a greater amount that you cannot show with laughing. You had to show it with crying somehow. I don't know if you ever experienced in your life being happy and cry for happiness, but that's what it always showed in us. It was like that. There was no end to tears. They were coming right and left every time you met somebody. You say, oh, you survived, you survived. An impossible task which came true. And I-- and I landed in Bratislava, where I met my friend from before who saw me on the street and came hugging me. I say, you saved my life. You saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you dummy. You were three years in Auschwitz? I was liberated in January. Look, you probably went through hell now for another four or five months, which I did. But anyway, that was meant to be. And that's what it was. So little by little, I-- I made my way back from Bratislava, like I said, on trucks and this time already on Russian vehicles. And-- and you know, a little food here, a little food there, until I came to Kosice. I mean, what I'm telling you right now took a few weeks. It wasn't in a few days because bridges were bombed out. It was all demolished and-- it was--.

speaker: interviewee
text: But everything was-- was-- was happiness. I mean, no matter what you endured now, there was-- it was all happiness. And-- and I come to Kosice, and I finally find a Russian soldier who said he'll take me to Michalovce. He's going there. But I didn't know that he charges. He thought I was in a Russian soldier. He'll probably takes you for free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few of the guys that I knew from my hometown too were also there. And he comes to Michalovce, and everybody goes down and gives him a few Czech crowns. I have no money. I have nothing on me. So I spoke a little Russian too, which by now I forgot. And I try to explain, look, I come from concentration camp. I says, everything today is supplied by us, food and transportation for free. That's what the UNRRA has decreed. I says, why would you want to charge me? I have no money. He pulls out his revolver, and he says he's going to shoot me if I don't pay him. So some of the other people who were on the truck too put together a few crowns that they gave him, and they're, like, released me. That was my one more chance to lose my life in that-- in that-- after the three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how I walked from the place where he dropped me off. I came to the house that we once owned. And I walk in in the yard, and I find the daughter of my uncle who was taken with me to Majdanek. She's there. We recognize each other. We started crying and hugging and kissing. And I ask her where is her mother. She says her mother left with-- with-- with one of the-- and her brother someplace, but she'll be soon back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I am in the-- in the-- in the house where I-- where we used to live. And she tells me that she gets now one room in our house by court order from a woman who occupies our house who took the house away when we left. And my aunt had to go to court to get permission from the court to order her to let her have one room because she's the nearest relative.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was hiding in the-- in the-- in the woods during the whole war, with the kids. So when the-- when the-- when my cousin tells me that, I was very furious. You have to remember, now, I'm coming back from a-- it's like a soldier from a battle front, you know, where killing by me right now who did me in would be nothing. I mean, I could right now take a knife and cut an SS man's throat in two seconds without pulling a muscle. That's how much hatred is in me. And here I hear that my aunt and my cousins had to obtain a court order to get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I walk into the house with the woman, and I say to her, where's an axe, in Slovakian language. She says, what do you need an axe? You give it to me. You'll find out in a minute what I need an axe for.

speaker: interviewee
text: Truthfully is that I wasn't going to hurt her with that axe. But I remember that, you know, I mentioned to you before I was standing there just in a pajama top, the pajama pants. That's all I came home, without any clothing. And I remember that my brothers hid in the attic a lot of clothing and a lot of soap and all kinds of stuff that were hidden between the ceiling and the floor. We had a third floor where you could walk upon/ And they used to pull the nails out with the boards, and in between the ceiling and the floors they used to hide a lot of boxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to dress up myself, so-- and I knew about it. So I took that axe. And when she gave me the axe, she ran away from the house. She never came back, by the way. She thought I was going to kill her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went up in the attic. And-- and I was really furious. I mean, I could have pulled all the nails and the boards out little by little and-- and make a decent job. But I took that axe, and I broke every piece of lumber there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I pulled out box by box of clothing that belonged to my sister that he had prepared for her-- for her wedding. Some bed sheets stuff. We found boxes of soap. A bar of soap after the war was like a piece of gold today, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found my oldest brother's, Meyer's, suits, shirts, ties, all business. So I took all these boxes, and I brought it down. By that time, my aunt came home already with her son. You can imagine how we were hugging and kissing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except she had very ill feeling towards me, which she told me later, because she felt that I was guilty that her husband was taken away by the mere fact--. You know, I was 17 years old. And when we went with these guys, every once in a while, they had an address. And every in a while, they would ask you, where does this guy live? Where does this guy live? Do you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- in my stupid head, not thinking of any, you know, serious effects that that would have, I might have pointed out one or two, and maybe my uncle too. And she had that feeling that I was the one who pointed out where the uncle lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had a little ill feelings. We were very close after the war until she died 10, 15 years ago. But that was-- that was when I-- when I came into the house, again, and I-- and I-- and I was back home whence I came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And stayed there for a while. My-- my brother came home. My middle brother Joe came home a few weeks later. And he brought along a friend who was also saved in concentration camp to stay with us. And then we heard some notice from people that my sister was alive and that she is in a certain city. So I picked myself up, and I went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] traveling again. OK. Let's pause here.
